Name: latency-aware_training-free_inference_via_spectral_stability
Title: Latency-Aware Training-Free Inference via Spectral Stability Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: Spectral stability diagnostics derived from PAC-Bayes generalization bounds can enable latency-aware, training-free inference by dynamically allocating compute resources based on activation geometry, minimizing hidden-state perturbation while enforcing strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Related work includes Spectral Latent Steering (zero-training test-time optimization via subspace projection), Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation (dynamic compute-throttling based on activation geometry), and Spectral Quantization Routing (precision allocation via activation covariance geometry). Our proposal differs by combining PAC-Bayes bounds with spectral stability diagnostics for latency-aware inference, introducing a novel Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework that minimizes hidden-state perturbation and enforces strict wall-clock budgets.
Abstract: We propose a latency-aware, training-free inference framework grounded in PAC-Bayes generalization bounds and spectral stability diagnostics. Our key insight is that spectral stability diagnostics derived from activation geometry can dynamically allocate compute resources, minimizing hidden-state perturbation while enforcing strict wall-clock budgets. We introduce a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework that targets ML inference theory, utilizing sequential or single-pass adaptive thresholds to avoid memory pressure from parallel sampling. We theoretically link spectral stability to PAC-Bayes generalization under perturbation, establishing representation geometry as a direct lever for system-scale latency optimization. Our experiments demonstrate superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, validating the computational tractability and scalability of our approach.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate spectral stability diagnostics on diverse benchmarks, measuring accuracy retention, subspace alignment stability, and inference efficiency.
Experiment 2: Compare throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers with state-of-the-art baselines on single-GPU setups.
Experiment 3: Validate computational tractability by measuring hidden-state perturbation overhead and memory-bandwidth usage.
Experiment 4: Test robustness under controlled distributional shifts, measuring accuracy retention and inference efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risk: Spectral stability diagnostics may not generalize across diverse benchmarks.
Limitation: The proposed framework relies on sequential or single-pass adaptive thresholds, which may limit scalability on multi-GPU setups.
Risk: Theoretical grounding may not fully capture practical implementation challenges.
